title: Heating your home without central heating
description: No boiler or radiators? Compare electric heaters, storage heaters, air-to-air heat pumps and heat-the-person tactics, with real per-hour UK costs.
blurb: Electric-only homes pay more for every unit of heat. Here is how to stay warm without a boiler, with real costs.
lede: Plenty of UK homes have no central heating at all. Some are flats with no gas supply, some are park homes or annexes, some have a boiler that died and an owner who is not ready to spend thousands replacing it. Whatever the reason, the problem is the same: every unit of heat now comes from electricity at roughly three and a half times the price of mains gas, so the habits that work in a gas-heated house will quietly empty your wallet here. The good news is that a home without central heating can still be warm and affordable. It just needs a different strategy, and that strategy is mostly about choosing where the heat goes, when you buy it, and how little of it you let escape.
short_answer: Without central heating, heat the person first and the room second. An electric blanket or heated throw costs 2p to 4p an hour, while a 2kW fan heater costs about 52p an hour at 26.11p per kWh. For whole-room heat, the cheapest electric options are an air-to-air heat pump at roughly 8p to 9p per kWh of heat delivered, or storage heaters charged on a cheap overnight tariff. Every other plug-in heater delivers heat at the full 26.11p per kWh, whatever the box claims.

sections.h2: Why no boiler changes the maths completely
sections.html:
Mains gas costs about 7.33p per kWh on the current price cap. Electricity costs about 26.11p per kWh. A gas boiler wastes a little of its fuel up the flue, so the real cost of gas heat lands around 8p per kWh of warmth in the room. Direct electric heat costs the full 26.11p. That gap is the single most important fact about living without central heating.

It means three things in practice:

  • You cannot afford to heat rooms nobody is sitting in. A gas household gets away with a warm hallway. At electric prices, empty rooms are a luxury.
  • The type of plug-in heater you buy barely matters for cost. A fan heater, an oil-filled radiator and an infrared panel all turn 1kWh of electricity into 1kWh of heat. Marketing that claims one is dramatically cheaper to run than another at the same output is wrong, and our energy myths guide explains why those claims keep appearing.
  • The two genuine exceptions, heat pumps and off-peak tariffs, are worth far more attention than heater shopping.

So the plan for an electric-only home runs in this order: cut the heat the building loses, heat your body cheaply, heat only the occupied room, and use a heat pump or cheap-rate electricity for any heavy lifting.

sections.h2: What each option actually costs per hour
sections.html:
Here is the honest comparison at the current cap rate. The last column is the one that matters: what you pay for each unit of heat that ends up in the room.

OptionTypical power drawCost per hour at full powerCost per kWh of heat
Electric blanket60Wabout 2p26.11p
Heated throw150Wabout 4p26.11p
Infrared panel600Wabout 16p26.11p
Halogen or radiant bar heater1.2kWabout 31p26.11p
Oil-filled radiator1.5kWabout 39p26.11p
Fan heater2kWabout 52p26.11p
Air-to-air heat pump (split unit)1kW in, around 3kW of heat outabout 26proughly 8p to 9p

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Heaters with thermostats cycle on and off, so real hourly costs are usually below the full-power figure once the room is up to temperature.

Notice that the blanket and the throw cost pennies because they draw so little power, not because they are more efficient. They put a small amount of heat exactly where you need it. The heat pump is the only entry that genuinely beats the 26.11p unit price, because it moves heat from outside air rather than generating it from scratch. For a fuller tour of the plug-in options, see electric heater types compared.

sections.h2: Heat the person before you heat the room
sections.html:
This is the cheapest trick in home heating and it matters most in an electric-only home. Warming a human costs a tiny fraction of warming the air around them.

  • An electric blanket at around 60W costs about 2p an hour. Run it for a whole evening and you have spent less than 10p.
  • A heated throw on the sofa draws around 150W, about 4p an hour, and keeps you comfortable in a room several degrees cooler than you would otherwise tolerate.
  • Layers do the same job for free. A decent jumper, slippers and a blanket let you drop the room temperature without dropping your comfort.
  • A hot water bottle made with a 2kWh-rated kettle boil costs about 3p and stays warm for hours.

The pattern is the same every time: a small, targeted heat source replaces a large, room-sized one. An evening under a heated throw plus a 600W panel ticking over costs around 20p. The same evening with a 2kW fan heater running half the time costs around 80p. Across a heating season that gap is well over £100 for one room alone.

None of this means living cold. It means spending your expensive electric heat on the half metre around your body first, then deciding how much whole-room heat you still want on top.

sections.h2: Storage heaters and off-peak tariffs: buying heat at night prices
sections.html:
If your home has storage heaters, or you are choosing a permanent system for a home that will stay electric, the way to escape the 26.11p unit rate is to buy electricity when it is cheap and store the heat.

Storage heaters charge a core of bricks overnight on an Economy 7 or similar two-rate tariff, then release the heat through the day. Night rates are typically around half the standard unit rate, though they vary by supplier and region, so check the rate on your own bill rather than trusting a rule of thumb. The method is simple: your night rate per kWh is roughly your cost per kWh of stored heat. If your night rate is 13p, your heating runs at half the price of a plug-in heater, which starts to close the gap on gas.

Two cautions. First, the day rate on a two-rate tariff is usually higher than the standard cap rate, so the tariff only pays if a good chunk of your usage genuinely shifts overnight. Second, older storage heaters leak heat all day whether you want it or not, and can leave you cold by evening. Modern high-retention models hold their charge far better and are worth the upgrade if storage heating is your long-term plan.

If you do not have storage heaters, the same logic applies to anything with thermal mass: some households on time-of-use tariffs run an oil-filled radiator or hot water cylinder on the cheap window. The principle is always the same, shift the load to the cheap hours and store the result.

sections.h2: Air-to-air heat pumps: the cheapest electric heat there is
sections.html:
A split air conditioning unit running in heating mode, properly called an air-to-air heat pump, is the one appliance that breaks the rule that electric heat costs 26.11p per kWh. Instead of converting electricity into heat, it uses electricity to move heat from the outside air into the room. In mild UK winter conditions a decent unit delivers around three units of heat for every unit of electricity, which works out at roughly 8p to 9p per kWh of warmth. That is essentially gas-boiler territory, achieved with nothing but a plug and an outdoor unit.

For a flat or small house with no gas supply, one or two split units heating the main living spaces is often the best value permanent answer. Installation for a single room typically runs to four figures rather than the five-figure cost of a full wet central heating system, there is no pipework, and the same unit cools the room in summer.

On grants: support schemes such as ECO4 can fund heating upgrades for eligible households, and the Boiler Upgrade Scheme supports air-to-water heat pumps, though air-to-air units sit outside some schemes. Eligibility rules and amounts change, so check the current position on the official GOV.UK pages before planning around any grant.

sections.h2: Cut the heat you need before buying any heater
sections.html:
Every kilowatt-hour of heat your home does not lose is a kilowatt-hour you do not buy at electric prices. In a gas house, a draughty door wastes 8p heat. In your house it wastes 26p heat, so the payback on cheap fixes is roughly three times faster.

  • Draught-proof the doors, letterbox, keyholes and any gappy floorboards in the rooms you actually heat. Most of this costs a few pounds per fix.
  • Close the doors of the heated room and keep the heat in one place. Heating one room well beats heating three rooms badly.
  • Thick curtains, closed at dusk, cut window losses sharply, and a cheap thermal liner does most of the work of an expensive pair.
  • Rugs on bare floors, radiator-style reflectors behind panel heaters on external walls, and a chimney draught excluder if you have an unused fireplace all follow the same logic.

Most of these fixes appear in our quick wins under a tenner list, and the seasonal jobs are in the winter energy checklist. One warning for the one-room strategy: unheated rooms in a cold house attract condensation and mould, so keep some background ventilation, wipe down cold windows, and let a little heat reach the coldest corners on damp weeks.

A home without central heating runs on a simple hierarchy. Stop the heat escaping first, because every saved unit is worth 26.11p. Heat your body for pennies with a blanket, a throw and decent layers. Heat only the room you are in, with whichever plug-in heater suits the job, since they all cost the same per unit of heat. And when you are ready to spend real money, put it into an air-to-air heat pump or modern storage heaters on an off-peak tariff, because those are the only two routes to heat that costs anything close to gas prices. Get the order right and an electric-only home is perfectly liveable. Get it wrong, with a 2kW fan heater roaring in every occupied room, and you will pay two to three times what a gas household pays for the same warmth. There is more on every option in our heating hub.

faq.q: What is the cheapest way to heat a home without central heating?
faq.a: An air-to-air heat pump is the cheapest whole-room option, delivering heat at roughly 8p to 9p per kWh because it moves heat rather than generating it. For occasional or personal heat, an electric blanket or heated throw at 2p to 4p an hour is far cheaper than any room heater. All ordinary plug-in heaters cost the same 26.11p per kWh of heat at the current cap rate.

faq.q: Which plug-in electric heater is cheapest to run?
faq.a: Per unit of heat, they are all identical: fan heaters, oil-filled radiators, infrared panels and halogen heaters all convert electricity to heat at close to 100 per cent efficiency, so each kWh costs about 26.11p. The differences are about fit: low-wattage panels suit small spaces and background warmth, radiant heaters warm people quickly in cold rooms, and oil-filled radiators with thermostats suit longer sessions.

faq.q: Is heating with electricity always more expensive than gas?
faq.a: Direct electric heating costs about 26.11p per kWh against roughly 8p per kWh for heat from a gas boiler, so yes for ordinary heaters. The exceptions are air-to-air heat pumps, which roughly match gas costs by delivering around three units of heat per unit of electricity, and storage heaters charged on a cheap overnight rate, which can get close.

faq.q: Can I get a grant if my home has no central heating?
faq.a: Possibly. ECO4 includes support for first-time central heating in eligible households, and other schemes support insulation and heat pumps. Eligibility usually depends on income, benefits and the home's energy rating, and the rules and amounts change regularly, so check the current schemes on GOV.UK or through your council before making plans.

faq.q: Is it safe to heat just one room and leave the rest cold?
faq.a: It is the right money-saving strategy, but cold rooms in a lived-in house collect moisture, which leads to condensation and mould. Keep trickle vents open or air rooms briefly each day, wipe condensation off cold windows, dry laundry with ventilation, and allow a little background heat into the coldest rooms during long damp spells.
